<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: procedural-memory-and-cognitive-loop-closure
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/procedural-memory-and-cognitive-loop-closure.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/procedural-memory-and-cognitive-loop-closure.qmd@41267f5a8
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Procedural Memory and Cognitive Loop Closure

The execution layer leaves traces everywhere: plans, jobs, edits, adapter
receipts, reviews, failures, residuals, replays, and repairs. Most of those
traces should remain history. Some should become warnings. A few should become
procedures.

Procedural memory is the discipline for deciding which is which.

The point is not to automate everything that repeats. That is how a system
turns one lucky path into future damage. The point is to let repeated work earn
reuse. A loop should close only when the stack can show comparable traces,
failed traces, near misses, invariant structure, parameters, preconditions,
postconditions, regression checks, monitoring signals, and retirement
triggers.

Memory becomes capability only when reuse carries the reason it was allowed.

## Problem

Repeated reasoning trajectories should not be improvised forever when they can
become verified procedures or tools.

The stack will see the same shapes of work again and again. A plan is
decomposed, a file is changed, a test fails, a patch is repaired, an approval
is requested, a result is reviewed, and a residual is recorded. Rebuilding that
entire path from scratch every time wastes attention and creates drift.

But raw repetition is not enough. A repeated trace can be a real procedure, a
fragile coincidence, a local habit, a contaminated workflow, or a warning that
the system keeps approaching the same failure. Procedural memory exists to
separate those cases.

The question is not "can this be automated?" The better question is "what
would make this safe to reuse?"

## Why Ordinary Memory Is Not Enough

A long context, retrieval store, or prompt template can help an agent remember.
It cannot by itself decide what should become operational machinery.

MemGPT is a useful external orientation point because it treats long-running
LLM systems as needing explicit memory tiers and control flow instead of
unbounded context. Procedural memory accepts that pressure but applies it to
workflows rather than only facts. A reusable procedure should not be a note
that says "do what worked last time." It should be a qualified record that
names when the procedure applies, when it does not apply, what evidence
supports it, and what retires it.

Repeated improvisation has one failure mode. Premature automation has another.
The first wastes cognition; the second creates brittle capability. An agent
that repeats a task from scratch may drift. An agent that promotes a single
appealing trace into a tool may lock in hidden assumptions.

The stack needs three modes to stay honest:

- interpretation mode for novel, ambiguous, or high-context work;
- compiled-tool mode for recurring procedures that have earned reuse;
- failsafe or reflex mode for narrow safety-critical responses.

Procedural memory is the gate between interpretation and compiled-tool mode.
It should make that transition explicit instead of letting convenience become
validation.

## Core Claim

Cognitive loop closure compiles repeated reasoning into verified parameterized
tools and procedural memory. In the live book, this remains a design rationale
at `argument` support.

Cognitive Loop Closure supplies the main vocabulary: trajectory logging, loop
detection, abstraction, active parameter discovery, tool synthesis,
verification, registry, routing, monitoring, revision, retirement, and tool
cards. RMI and RGS connect loop closure to ratcheting systems, benchmark
pressure, residual escrow, and regression preservation. Benchmaxxing adds
benchmark lifecycle and anti-Goodhart discipline. Talos supplies typed
execution traces, audit, replay, feedback, and residual handling. Project
Theseus sources supply implementation-lineage pressure around report-first
tool closure and operator-visible work surfaces. MoECOT remains
connector/source-note context until concrete runtime artifacts, code, logs,
readiness records, or benchmark results are imported and inspected.

The repository currently validates the shape of a procedural tool record and
implements finite Lean predicates for two record-level gates: a generated tool
must record source traces, parameters, and verification, and a failed
regression blocks routable promotion. That is useful record discipline. It is
not a loop detector, tool synthesizer, regression run, routing monitor,
retirement automation, Talos loop-closure pipeline, MoECOT runtime
reproduction, or Theseus execution replay.

## Mechanism

Loop closure begins after execution has left enough comparable trace to learn
from. Artifact graphs provide the raw material: job paths, adapter effects,
edits, approvals, tests, failures, residuals, replay limits, and review
outcomes.

Procedural memory turns that material into a promotion path.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Trace["Candidate traces"] --> Cluster["Comparable loop cluster"]
  Cluster --> Negative["Failures and near misses"]
  Negative --> Invariant["Invariant abstraction"]
  Invariant --> Params["Parameter discovery"]
  Params --> Candidate["Tool candidate"]
  Candidate --> Verify["Verification and regressions"]
  Verify --> Gate{"Qualified reuse?"}
  Gate -- "yes" --> Card["Routable procedure card"]
  Gate -- "no" --> Residual["Quarantine, residual, or retire"]
  Card --> Monitor["Monitoring and retirement trigger"]
  Residual --> Monitor
```

The order matters. A trace does not become a tool because it looks elegant. It
first joins a comparable loop cluster. The cluster includes successful traces,
failed traces, near misses, rejected cases, and limiting conditions. The stack
then asks what stayed invariant, what changed, which parameters matter, which
preconditions are real, and which postconditions can be checked.

Only then is there a tool candidate.

The candidate still cannot become routable until verification and regression
checks pass. If the checks fail, the result is not hidden inside a polished
procedure. It becomes quarantine, residual, revision, or retirement.

That is the difference between memory and habit. Habit repeats. Procedural
memory explains why reuse is allowed.

## Procedure Qualification

The key artifact is a Procedure Qualification Packet around the smaller
procedural tool record.

It should name:

- candidate traces;
- rejected traces;
- near misses;
- abstraction rationale;
- parameter-discovery notes;
- preconditions;
- postconditions;
- verification result;
- regression floor;
- SCF target;
- authority requirement;
- runtime tier;
- risk tier;
- monitoring signal;
- residuals;
- retirement trigger;
- explicit non-claims.

The packet is not a badge of success. It is the evidence a gate needs in order
to say yes, no, quarantine, revise, or retire.

Negative examples are not clutter. They are the boundary of the procedure. A
loop detector that sees only successful traces will overgeneralize. A tool
card that omits near misses cannot explain where the procedure stops. A router
that cannot see failures will reuse the tool outside its envelope.

Procedural memory therefore keeps failures attached to reuse. A useful
procedure is narrower than the traces that inspired it until evidence
justifies widening the scope.

## Interfaces

Procedural memory sits between execution and routing.

Artifact graphs supply traces. Runtime adapters supply effect receipts.
Readiness gates decide whether a candidate can move into routable status.
Stable capability fields give the generated procedure a capability boundary.
Benchmark ratchets preserve the floor the procedure must not break. Routing
heads select qualified tools only under the recorded scope.

This interface prevents a private shortcut from becoming an ambient
capability. A procedure can be useful and still not be routable. It may lack a
regression floor, carry stale preconditions, require authority the current job
does not have, depend on a retired SCF, or preserve a residual that blocks
promotion.

The procedure card should be easy for a future router to reject.

## Invariants

Only verified loops become routable tools.

Comparable traces come before synthesis.

Parameters, assumptions, preconditions, and postconditions are explicit.

Negative examples stay attached to the reusable procedure.

Failed regressions block routable promotion.

Promotion records residuals instead of erasing them.

Retirement triggers are part of the tool definition, not later maintenance
notes.

These invariants are the desired discipline. The repository currently checks
only finite record predicates and a public procedural-tool fixture; it does
not enforce the full procedure-qualification lifecycle in a running system.

## Failure Modes

Premature toolification occurs when one successful trace becomes a reusable
tool before comparable cases, failures, parameters, and regression checks are
known.

Anecdote closure occurs when an appealing example is treated as a loop.

Hidden-assumption closure occurs when a procedure works only because an
unstated local condition happened to hold.

Procedural memory poisoning occurs when bad traces, contaminated data, or
unsafe shortcuts become reusable machinery.

Route drift occurs when a verified tool is used outside its SCF, benchmark
floor, authority envelope, or retirement condition.

Tool immortality occurs when a once-valid procedure keeps running after
sources change, APIs shift, governance rules tighten, or regressions fail.

Each failure should keep the candidate in interpretation mode, quarantine,
residual review, revision, or retirement. None should be hidden by calling the
procedure "memory."

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a public trace-to-tool record discipline:
a procedural tool schema, a valid fixture, finite promotion predicates, and a
small synthetic trace set that exercises both promotion and rejection.

The repository already has the first layer. The procedural tool record fixture
checks source traces, invariant structure, parameters, preconditions,
postconditions, verification result, risk/runtime tiers, monitoring plan,
residuals, regressions, lifecycle state, and retirement criteria.
`AsiStackProofs.ProceduralMemory` contains finite record predicates that a
generated tool records its source traces, parameters, and verification result,
and that a failed regression blocks routable promotion.

The next honest slice would be deliberately small: three successes, one near
miss, one failure, and one extracted tool candidate. The harness should prove
that the candidate cites all source traces, narrows its scope around the
failure, preserves the near miss, and remains unroutable until regression
checks pass.

It should also exercise rejection: a missing near miss, stale precondition,
failed benchmark floor, unapproved authority requirement, or retired SCF keeps
the tool in quarantine or retired state.

That would still not be a deployed loop-closure system. It would be a
public-safe proof-of-discipline that reuse can be denied.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a procedure foundry for verified reusable cognition.

Such a foundry would mine artifact graphs, job receipts, adapter receipts,
failures, residuals, and replay records. It would cluster comparable work,
preserve successes and failures, abstract invariant structure, discover
parameters, synthesize candidate procedures, verify regressions, bind each
candidate to stable capability fields and readiness gates, attach monitoring,
and retire stale tools.

The product-level system would make reusable cognition stateful:

- candidate trace;
- loop cluster;
- tool candidate;
- verified reuse;
- routable tool;
- quarantined tool;
- retired tool.

A trace could not jump directly to routable behavior. A procedure could not
escape its evidence envelope. A router could not treat memory as an ambient
toolbox. A benchmark pass could not erase residuals. A once-good tool could
not live forever without monitoring and retirement.

That endpoint remains unimplemented here. Procedural memory stays at
`argument` support until trace sets, loop detectors, tool candidates,
regression harnesses, readiness gates, monitoring logs, retirement traces, and
public-safe implementation records show that the stack can learn from
repetition without letting repetition become ungoverned habit.

## Evidence Boundary

This chapter argues for governed loop closure. It does not claim that the ASI
Stack currently generates verified tools from repeated work.

Current evidence consists of source synthesis, passage-reviewed source
mappings, source-noted external memory context, a public procedural-tool
fixture, and finite Lean record predicates. Those artifacts support the shape
of the promotion path. They do not prove loop detection, invariant
abstraction, active parameter discovery, synthesized tool correctness,
regression quality, routing monitor behavior, retirement automation, Talos
loop-closure behavior, MoECOT runtime behavior, Project Theseus replay, or
autonomous self-improvement.

The chapter's own discipline is the same as the mechanism it describes:
closure is allowed only where the evidence actually closes the loop.

## Handoff

Procedural memory completes the planning, memory, reasoning, and execution
loop. Once procedures can be qualified, the next problem is selection.

Routing Heads and Specialist Cores opens that next layer. It treats tools,
procedures, specialists, and routes as candidates for governed selection, not
as ambient capabilities available to every task. Procedural memory supplies
the qualified candidates; routing decides when, where, and whether they should
be used.
